author: @paulscott6463
text: Robert, I am so happy to hear mention build a dam in the sea and let it fill up and drain out, I have been talking about this for years to anyone that will sit long enough and let me bore them. I live up in West Cumbria, where we have 5-8 meter tides, twice a day, I have a long held belief that if there was a subsea network of turbines built on the sea bed from st bees to Kirkcudbright, that would be approx 50 miles x 50 miles x 35 miles, of tidal basin you could control, I would love to hear your thoughts on the possibility of this, the energy outputs, do we have the technology to really harness this, I believe we do, anyways, glad I have passed that idea on, ohh I also thing maybe a large dam built across the solway first, with hundreds  of turbines on the seabed, below the dam, then you can close some off to maintain etc , and use something like a large sea lock (london flood barrier type) for shipping to pass, and sealife to pass through at high tides etc.

author: @salaciouscreations4323
text: I used your videos to make a solution for winter in the long term.   5lbs of wood burns for 1 hour.  500ml of methanol burns for 1 hour.    Put your burner into a electric fire frame I got from b and q.   Made a brick heat exchanger.   And added sand batterys to store the heat from the fire the hour burn gives me 3 hours of heat.     Worked out £200 should get me through to spring in terms of fuel cost.    So thank you.  I made 3 of them as soon as I work out something on one then I do it to the others.     Converting some of that heat to a electric current to add to the internal fire sand batterys  just to make the batteries a bit more effective in collecting heat with your copper wire stuff.     I did have issues at first and still have no idea on how people are getting 5 hours of heat out of the methanol heater but what I have works well and has been keeping me and my family warm so far and we already had -1°c temps.   So your a credit to the UK mate.

author: @troyhonaker3516
text:
very pleasant guy who understands man is basically good.  I caught that.

We do have some evil cats afoot, trying to crush society, but overall we have good people.

We have plenty of energy available, we just need to use it better.

Is there a true, right now, crisis.  No.  This is over exaggerated by those who would profit off our suppression.

Now, obviously that does not mean we don’t improve.  After all, we do have to live here.

The key thing is to stay mobile, but use our resources better.  Clean up the earth, plant trees, etc.

I kinda see coppicing and pollarding as a great way to use out resources and maintain habitat.  What is ash from burning?  It’s fertilizer.  What are the gases from burning?  More food for trees.  The natural cycle is still there.  Combine this with sand batteries and other things.  The right trees are wonderful resources and you don’t have to level forests to use the resource.  And you get new upshoots after resources are taken.

I just don’t think it is necessary to lock down humans.  This is a dream of an oppressor.  The change can be more gradual than that.  We can certainly carpool and ride bikes some.  Trees can be planted.

I don’t think we have to give up much.

So much potential energy on earth.

But, humans must not be pulled apart, otherwise we die as humanity.

Thank you Robert, your thoughts and opinions were very well put forth and reasonable.  There is too much of a schism in Humanity these days and my son constantly argues the potential of the human race if they would just calm down, work together, and help each other.  In my life i have worn many hats.  Car mechanic, IT professional and so on.  In every case it is use the right tool for the job.  I appreciate your expanding on that to that this applies to every aspect of life.  Yes a butter knife will substitute as a screw driver.  but the screwdriver will always outperform the butter knife.  

If I may also ask a question, I am trying to build a rocket stove for burning wood chips/pellets.  I have tried many different things from other stoves I have seen on various media.  biggest problem is the baskets I am making to hold the material either get choked with ash or the material falls through and blocks the airway.  What are some alternatives that you would propose to alleviate this problem?

Many thanks to  you for  your content, wisdom and willingness to test and share your knowledge.

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
I've often thought that one of the cheapest ways to make better use of room heating would be to take advantage of the difference in temperature at ceiling and floor level. Usually only a a couple of degrees but something as simple as a drainpipe and a computer fan ( 1- 2 Watts so could easily be set to run off a small solar panel and battery ) could redistribute that, even if it only make 1/2 degree difference it take some pressure off of the rooms current heating system.

Not too dissimilarly most people are running around 100-250W of fridge/freezers, a small fan run off a thermoelectric device, attached to the heat exchange unit on those, could be used as forced cooling to the heat exchanger, improving both the efficiency of the fridge/freezer and potentially blowing that heat where it'd be more useful.

author: @pauljenkinson1452
text:
This is a good video with some wisdom thrown in. There are some corrections / additional comments, see below:
1. Electroculture has produced huge amounts of food without fertiliser so this would end any food crisis
2. Developed countries waste food in overproduction and poor storage with undeveloped countries waste a lot of food not being able to get it out of the ground.
3. The birth rate to keep a stable population is 2.1 but it has been 1.8 for some time. Since excess deaths have been increasing recently to 5 times pandemic levels, it won't be long the population growth lag due to life span becomes a decline.
4. To end the boom and bust cycle then money needs to return to gold due to its limited supply. This would also end products having short design life's caused by the cheap printing of fiat money.
5. There is a global cabal aided by the vast witless common folk. JFK called it a "Monolithic and ruthless conspiracy" and Karen Hudes called it the "Network of Global Corporate Control". Ignoring that is a huge mistake and it's this corruption we need to clean up.

I was one of a few that requested the House of Commons to replace the collapsing fiat money for gold in the Global Debt Facility offered by the Board of Governors of the World Bank and IMF, the MP's remained silent and refused to tell their constituencies. Until that happens all MP's are corrupt in my eyes.

author: @winstonsmith935
text:
Bill Gates's Depopulation Initiative [U.N Agenda 21] May 16 2011 and CO2 and Global Warming.
In a recent TED conference presentation, Microsoft billionaire Bill Gates, who has donated hundreds of millions of dollars to new vaccine efforts, speaks on the issue of CO2 emissions and its effects on climate change. He presents a formula for reducing CO2 emissions as follows: CO2 = P x S x E x C.
P = People
S = Services per person
E = Energy per service
C = CO2 per energy unit

Then he adds that in order to get CO2 to zero, "probably one of these numbers is going to have to get pretty close to zero."

Following that, Bill Gates begins to describe how the first number — P (for People) — might be reduced.  How? Vaccines.
You and Gates are different?

author: @charlespenny9611
text:
This video didn't cover any of the cool generation/storage/application things that I subscribed to your channel for, and yet I think it is my favorite of your videos!

Sometimes I feel like being hopeful for a better future puts me in the minority among my peers. I see cycles between urgent panic for immediate changes and a sort of indignant resignation to the way things are. To me it has always seemed that sustainable improvements to the world take time. And that time makes it seem slow to human perception. A decade to reach a milestone seems like too long to a lot of people, even if in the context of history it is incredibly quick, and with relation to the scope and scale of problems being solved it is ultimately adequate.

I have never heard you mention entropic hydro-electric power. You have come up with amazing work-arounds like the plumbers supplies rocket stove  and carbon infra-red heaters that had me going "yes! Just what I should make for my off-grid home", through to modern takes on forgotten ideas.

The entropic hydro power station taps the vast energy available wherever fresh water meets salt water and so entropy rises. To my knowledge only one pilot plant as ever been built, by a Norwegian engineer. What is needed is imaginative side-steps of the engineering constraints of tapping this vast resource that countries like Norway, your's and mine, that have long coastlines, scattered settlements and abundant fresh water flow into the sea.

It would seem you are an ideal person to investigate.
Cheers

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text:
Wow ! This is (so far) the only video , where I partially disagree  , although , you did made some solid points . You are right (in my opinion) about resources management and decentralization of electric generation (where it is possible). However , people are not inherently good or bad - they are just people. That means (with exception of mental deviance , like sociopaths and narcissists..) , people qualities are dictated by circumstances (of growing up and living) , mentality , culture... Given a chance , most people would be "good" , but not all of them and , even than , the term "good" isn't applicable to everyone in the world , in a same way and/or time - it is a matter of perspective and circumstances. Evil companies don't exist - what exists , are pretty dehumanized organizations , without any moral , or ethic consideration , with lots of money and influence (goes with money). Interests aren't driven by bettering the world , only by bottom line figure (though , companies like to advertise their "care" abut potential customers and environment and everything else trendy) . In combination with Neo colonialism and globalism - we have , what we have. Ideologists in politics are very , very rare animals (almost non existent) and extremely rarely (historically) can make a difference (not always with positive results) . Occupational choices (police , medicine , etc..) are very rarely driven by some noble ideas (due to a human nature and circumstances) , but , occasionally , there is some truth in that.  Energy crisis is totally artificial , because resources are still there , but some people decided to express their "virtue" to the other like minded people and persuaded their respective populations , that it is  the only "right" thing to do , while nobody in that class of people would be affected at all . I agree about consumerism , but that is hardly a whole world illness (unless you were talking about developed and , mostly , western countries). 
I do agree that change should come from within individuals , but , historically , that change can only come after some "world changing" events. I hope that such event would not be with radioactive and/or biochemical consequences , this time. In my mind - it is all about control (over people , at least).  Knowledge is power , but only if people act upon it .

author: @HexBeach
text:
Some good thoughts there, so I just want to say it seems there is a level of interference in UK electricity prices.

I've lost the reference, but roughly the UK wholesale price is fixed by the *highest* generation method in use at any time. I forget when the contract ends, but until then someone, somewhere is making higher profits when coal is being burned (the worst for NOx and CO2 as well).

Price caps, subsidies and any form of manipulation can often seem as though they help, then end up making things worse.

author: @trjsjackson89
text:
Agree with your points. I think that there is often a herd mentality to problem solving. We look at someone else's work and get locked onto to using their method when a better idea may be staring us in the face.    
One idea that I think would be an easy way to reduce individual grid dependence would be to make use of solar to heat water for our household uses. Certainly their are climates that would be less ideal, but here in the southwest US a substantial impact could be made to our electric cost. Excess hot water could be pumped into a sand battery for later use. Space heating could also be supplemented with the hot water. Watching your channel has opened my eyes to all sorts of things that can be fun and worthwhile projects to investigate...where to begin!

author: @musikSkool
text:
1: Greatly increase availability to residential bio-produced electricity.  Biomass, homemade solar, electricity producing appliances, pedal power to charge cellphones, etc.  I want it as easy to buy appliances that make homemade electricity/biofuel as it is to buy a laundry washing machine or a toaster.

2: Greatly increase the cost of energy.  This sounds bad, but just think of all the people who will start to use homemade electricity.  People will have a machine, about the size of a refrigerator, that they come home, put some yard waste in, and out comes enough electricity to do their daily commute, laundry, and charge electronics.  Unless the price of electricity goes up, there will never be any motivation for people to be part of the solution.

Why do very few people grow potatoes in their backyard?  Because a sack of potatoes is a few bob.  In WWII the availability of food was so low, that the price of food went very high, so it was financially responsible to grow your own dinner.  Now imagine all the people living in the country who could be growing biomass crops so that people in the city can do their laundry.  People who are able to will naturally start hanging out laundry to dry when it costs them to use a machine to dry it.

"1:" needs to happen first.  People need to be able to simply do things for themself, or else society will collapse when energy becomes scarce.  We need to use the relatively plentiful fuel now to prepare for when it wont be plentiful.  Someday it will cost so much energy to manufacture a residential biomass electricity generator that it will simply not happen.  People will have to scrap together unresearched, unimproved, inefficient, dangerous, unreliable, systems in their garage without the use of power tools.  Instead of that, lets start churning out clean, well-researched, efficient, reliable, safe, appliances that anyone can use.  You need to buy a flashlight and batteries BEFORE the power goes out, or you will be in the dark.  There is no point driving to the shop after it happens, since many shops don't accept money in a power outage, and you might not have cash on hand because you usually use your card.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Robert, this was such a really great discussion on the many theories and I am really glad how you handled them. Well done.
We use a large water dam in the region. The large amount of water can be used to power homes for quite some time. When there isn't enough power generation, then they open the dam and this provides hydroelectric power. When there is an overproduction of electricity due to lack of demand, they literally pump water from the lower reservoirs back above the dam for later use. 
This method is much better than "lithium battery" simply because the quantity of energy that has to be stored is immense. In that respect, it is an achievable solution for the scale of the requirement. I can definitely see Solar, Wind and Geothermal as perfect compliments to this method of storage because these method can generate electricity which can then be used to supply the grid or to pump water back into the reservoirs.

author: @totherarf
text:
I think you are wrong to talk in the singular Rob. It is Always problems and answers. Often the real problem turns out to be how to order their relative importance. 

If I can offer the answer (or at least one answer) to one of the problems it might be a step forwards. Population! Yes it is a Big one and getting bigger!
There has been an observable correlation between family size and educational levels!
More precisely Female education. It would seem that the more we educate women the less kids they have!
The solution should be blindingly obvious, especially as we live in the information age. Give more women access to more education and remove barriers to them accessing it!

The next problem will be How well did we do that and how much lag is there between the education increase and the family size reduction? That one I do not know.

I suspect (without any empirical evidence) that most viewers of this channel are males, so this may not be affecting a reduction directly, even though it is eminently educational in nature. However there is a ripple effect. This ripple effect may well reach further than we think ....... I do hope so!

author: @technosaurus3805
text:
We are using less than 1% of earth's renewable resources and of that we are less than 10% efficient.
We use essentially 0% of the ocean, 0% of the underground, and inefficiently underuse the small percentage of the surface. The earth can easily support 8 trillion humans even without genetically engineered humans or foods simply by using it better, wasting less and allowing free markets to act on the opportunities as they become profitable.
Floating gardens and housing across the oceans.
Underground houses and mushroom farms with gardens on top.
Switch to highland cattle breeds for meat to open up more farmable land for crops.
*Capture more waste for recycling and reuse*
A separate urine waste pipeline could divert tons of urea fertilizer and solid waste is already in use as a manure replacement with minimum treatment... other waste water could be used for watering the local flora with proper controls.
Trash can now easily be stripped of useful recyclables then burned for electricity and the fly ash and clinker used to make concrete... almost 0 waste.
We already have the tech and/or engineering capacity to do it, but no overwhelming incentive... yet. Climate alarmism, intentionally sped up by order(s) of magnitude, is intended to scare people to artificially create that incentive - unfortunately it also discourages the most rewarding/effective endeavor to create a better future, parenthood. See Adams law of slow moving disasters.

author: @adamcrofts58
text: Being a humanist myself I totally agree with you. All my family have a make do and mend attitude. What I am interested in is not changing the world per se but trying to extract all the potential energy out of the heat that I have to use. I had a small bag of fine river sand that I use for my fish tank, so I placed it in the bottom of my bath whilst having one. After half an hour I dried the bag off and placed it on top of a thermometer. I got a reading of 33 degrees c. It dropped slowly over the next couple of hours to 22 degrees c. My point being a hot bath that you empty goes down the drain and all that potential heat with it. Small things add up. It's not what you have but what you do with it. Again thanks for your shows, they always give me so many ideas. Keep up the good work.

author: @Sindrijo
text:
I recommend having a look at Hans Roslings' (the late) lecture on population growth and where we are heading. My own take-away from it was that we need to put as much effort as possible into reducing poverty in the developing world and one of the most effective ways to do that is increasing education across the board, the population growth in the western world has basically stopped and it's only the developing world that is growing in population at break-neck speed. The population growth in Asia is already starting to slow considerably so that leaves basically only the continent of Africa which is estimated to add about 2-3 billion people before the end of the century. Most estimates calculate that the world's population will peak around 12 billion where it will probably stay until humanity finds new planets to live on.

You are correct in saying that there isn't one solution to our energy problems, no magic bullet, we need to harness nature in every possible and sustainable way to wean ourselves off dirty energy sources. That means trying out all kinds of potential solutions in various locations and environments.

It also means we need to re-evaluate a lot of the 'arbitrary' things we do due to culture. One such thing is the amount of meat we consume because it is a very inefficient way of feeding the population. I don't think we will be stopping any time soon, but I do hope that it is possible to change it so that meat is no longer considered the cheap commodity that it has become today. We have a significant percentage of the population that consumes meat in some capacity almost every single day, that isn't sustainable! In my opinion meat should become a great occasion again. The only other solution is if the energy efficiency of 'lab-grown meat' can approach other foods. I seriously doubt the cultural value of 'lab grown meat' is worth talking about.


It's not just technology we need, we also need cultural shifts that allow the best technological solutions that are the most sustainable to succeed. I worry that we won't be able to transition quickly enough.

author: @richbuilds_com
text:
I'm a humanist too. Your second point: "Corporations are evil" is the truth. Capitalism got us out of the stone age into the technical age but it needs to die here as it's unsustainable.

CAPITALism is only about gaining capital/resources. The individual corps get bigger as they squeeze out the smaller. 

The *only* end game for that scenario is resource scarcity for everyone who isn't big corp. And as they hold all the resources the imbalance is impossible to fix.

author: @brianknowles7130
text: I think part of the problem that people don't commonly understand. is that it's easy enough to make electricity [ almost any fool can do this ] the problem is .. the storage .. of it ! As yet, we ' the world' have not yet invented a system to store electricity in huge quantities so it's available to every home & business when it's required and the constant supply. We don't want fossil fuels, we don't want nuclear fuel, people don't want wind turbines etc. etc. but .. we all want to flick a switch and get heating & lighting. tends to make me smile ... rgds

author: @malHHkenny
text: People who advocate population limit, see no downside. Except that they typically advocate limiting birth-rate below replacement level (below 2.1 per parent couple), which fails to consider the way the population then ages -- straining resources while losing productivity. (Moving such social engineering to the later age-group just shifts to other kinds of ethical problems. Not to mention loss that occurs when experience closes.)
